items.question: Define Shannon entropy H(X) for a discrete random variable and explain what it measures.
items.answer: H(X) = −Σ_x p(x) log p(x), using a stated log base (usually 2 for bits). It is the expected self-information −log p(x)—the average surprise in one draw from X. Low H means outcomes are predictable; high H means outcomes are harder to guess in advance.

items.question: A fair coin and a biased coin with P(H)=0.9 each have two outcomes. Compare their entropies in bits.
items.answer: Fair coin: H = −0.5 log₂ 0.5 − 0.5 log₂ 0.5 = 1 bit (maximum for two outcomes). Biased coin: H ≈ −0.9 log₂ 0.9 − 0.1 log₂ 0.1 ≈ 0.47 bits. The biased coin is more predictable, so average surprise is lower.

items.question: Why is entropy never negative for discrete X, and when does it equal zero?
items.answer: Each term −p log p is non-negative because 0 < p ≤ 1 implies log p ≤ 0. Summing non-negative terms gives H(X) ≥ 0. Equality H(X) = 0 occurs only when one outcome has p = 1 and all others have p = 0—the variable is deterministic.

items.question: Explain information gain in one sentence and give a numeric example.
items.answer: Information gain is the drop in label entropy after a split: H(parent) minus weighted H(children). Example: parent classes [50, 50] have H = 1 bit; a split into pure nodes [40,0] and [0,10] yields child H = 0, so gain = 1 bit.

items.question: How does Shannon entropy H(P) relate to cross-entropy H(P, Q)?
items.answer: H(P) = −Σ p(x) log p(x) uses the true distribution for both weighting and log. Cross-entropy H(P, Q) = −Σ p(x) log q(x) measures expected surprise under model Q when data follow P. When Q = P, H(P, Q) = H(P). Otherwise H(P, Q) ≥ H(P), with the gap equal to KL divergence D_KL(P || Q).
